ontology.foodex2_description: Enchiladas — corn tortilla filled dish with sauce; vegetarian variant mapped to closest FoodEx2 composite dish anchor
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for vegetarian enchiladas specifically; A0FKP (composite cereal-based dish) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for enchiladas or vegetarian enchiladas as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of vegetarian enchiladas consists of two rolled corn tortillas filled with beans, vegetables, and cheese, topped with enchilada sauce, weighing approximately 250 g.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetarian Enchiladas — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Database, record G00010839
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo canonical record G00010839 for Vegetarian Enchiladas. Energy 33 kcal/100g is notably low and likely reflects a sauce-dominant or partial-component measurement; values are preserved as reported. Saturated fat not reported in HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 21318 (cheese enchilada, restaurant-prepared) as closest approved analogous source for saturated fat only.

gi_source.title: Corn tortilla and bean-based composite dish GI — analogous estimate based on corn tortilla (GI ~52) and black beans (GI ~30) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for vegetarian enchiladas in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: corn tortilla (GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database) and black beans (GI ~30, University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted composite GI estimated at approximately 52 given tortilla dominance. GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × available carbohydrate per serving (3.12 g/100g × 250 g serving = 7.8 g) / 100 = 4.1.

hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 144 mg/100g is below the 200 mg/100g threshold of concern; however, enchilada sauce and cheese can vary widely in sodium content, so patients with hypertension should request low-sodium sauce and limit added cheese to keep total sodium within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate 3.12 g/100g; dietary fibre 1.1 g/100g; estimated GI ~52
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate density (3.12 g/100g as reported) and estimated low GI (~52 based on corn tortilla and black bean components) make this dish suitable for blood glucose management, but patients should note that a full restaurant portion of enchiladas will deliver substantially more carbohydrate than this per-100g figure suggests; corn tortillas are preferred over flour tortillas for a lower glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat ~0.9 g/100g; cholesterol 6 mg/100g; sodium 144 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and cholesterol content make vegetarian enchiladas a heart-friendly option when prepared with minimal cheese and low-sodium sauce; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and avoid high-fat cheese toppings to maintain a favourable lipid profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade ingredients: tomatoes, bell peppers, chili powder
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bell peppers, tomatoes, and chili-based enchilada sauce are nightshade-derived ingredients that may trigger inflammatory responses in RA-sensitive individuals; patients with confirmed nightshade sensitivity should request nightshade-free fillings (e.g., zucchini, mushrooms, spinach) and an alternative sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Enchiladas are a Mexican-origin dish consisting of corn tortillas rolled around a filling of black beans, cheese, bell peppers, onion, and tomatoes, then topped with a chili-based enchilada sauce and baked until the cheese melts. The dish delivers a savoury, mildly spicy flavour with a soft tortilla texture and a rich, tangy sauce. As recorded in the Singapore HPB Foodo database (record G00010839), the per-100g nutritional profile shows 33 kcal, 0.67 g protein, 2.15 g fat, and 3.12 g carbohydrate — values that are notably low and likely reflect a sauce-dominant or partial-component measurement rather than a full assembled portion. Dietary fibre is 1.1 g/100g and sodium is 144 mg/100g. The dish is vegetarian and contains dairy (cheese) and gluten (tortilla). The estimated glycaemic index of approximately 52 (low GI) is based on the corn tortilla and black bean components. Clinically, the dish is suitable for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes management at moderate portion sizes, provided low-sodium sauce and limited cheese are used. Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution due to the nightshade ingredient burden (tomatoes, bell peppers, chili). The dish is commonly served as a lunch or dinner item and is popular in Mexican and Tex-Mex cuisine contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in vegetarian enchiladas?
What is the GI of enchiladas?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Foodo canonical record G00010839 (confidence 0.95 per source metadata), but the very low energy value of 33 kcal/100g raises a plausibility concern suggesting the record may reflect a sauce-only or partial-component measurement rather than a fully assembled enchilada dish. Saturated fat was estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component data. Overall confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. The per-100g energy of 33 kcal is unusually low for a fully assembled enchilada dish (typical assembled enchiladas: ~150–200 kcal/100g), suggesting this HPB record may represent the enchilada sauce component or a diluted preparation. Values are preserved as reported per source fidelity rules. Users and clinicians should apply caution when using these values for full-dish portion estimation. 2. Saturated fat (0.9 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 21318 (cheese enchilada, restaurant-prepared) as the HPB record does not report saturated fat. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this HPB vegetarian enchiladas record are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on corn tortilla (GI ~52) and black beans (GI ~30) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for vegetarian enchiladas was found. 5. The food name in the input record contains a typographical error ('Enchladas'); the canonical spelling 'Enchiladas' is used in food_name; the original spelling is preserved as an alias. 6. This dish is classified as Western/Mexican cuisine served in Singapore context; multilingual Singaporean naming requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean-origin dish — it is a Mexican dish served in Singapore.
